background: A random sample of 395 people were surveyed. Each person was asked their interest in riding a bicycle (Variable A) and their age (Variable B). The data that resulted from the survey is summarized in Table 3.10.

task.query: Is there evidence to conclude, at the 0.05 level, that the desire to ride a bicycle depends on age? In other words, is age independent of the desire to ride a bicycle?
task.answer:
Here $r=2$ and $c=4$. The expected number in each cell can be obtained as shown in Table 3.11:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E_{11}=\frac{1}{395}\left(\sum_{i=1}^2 O_{i 1}\right)\left(\sum_{j=1}^4 O_{1 j}\right)=\frac{1}{395}(100)(201)=50.8861 \
& E_{12}=\frac{1}{395}\left(\sum_{i=1}^2 O_{i 2}\right)\left(\sum_{j=1}^4 O_{1 j}\right)=\frac{1}{395}(98)(201)=49.8684 \
& E_{13}=\frac{1}{395}(99)(201)=50.3772 \
& \ldots \
& E_{23}=\frac{1}{395}(99)(194)=48.6228 \
& E_{24}=\frac{1}{395}(98)(194)=48.1316
\end{aligned}
$$

The test statistic value

$$
\lambda_{v a l u e}^2=\sum_{i=1}^2 \sum_{j=1}^4 \frac{\left(O_{i j}-E_{i j}\right)^2}{E_{i j}}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{(60-50.8861)^2}{50.8861}+\frac{(54-49.8684)^2}{49.8684}+\ldots+\frac{(57-48.1316)^2}{48.1316} \
& =8.0062
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\alpha=0.05$. So $\chi_{0.05,(2-1)(4-1)}^2=\chi_{0.05,3}^2=7.815$. Since $\chi_{\text {value }}^2=8.0062>$ 7.815 we, therefore, reject the null hypothesis. That is, there is sufficient evidence, at the 0.05 level, to conclude that the desire to ride a bicycle depends on age.
